---
format:
  html: default
  typst:
    output-file: all-theorems-typst.pdf
  revealjs:
    output-file: all-theorems-revealjs.html
    slide-level: 4
  pdf: default
---

::: {#thm-fund}
## Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
Every integer greater than 1 has a unique factorization into primes.
:::

::: {#lem-pigeon}
## Pigeonhole
If $n+1$ items are placed in $n$ boxes, some box contains at least two items.
:::

::: {#cor-prime}
## Infinitely Many Primes
There is no largest prime.
:::

::: {#prp-even}
## Even Squares
If $n$ is even, then $n^2$ is divisible by 4.
:::

::: {#cnj-twin}
## Twin Prime Conjecture
There are infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p + 2$ is also prime.
:::

::: {#alg-euclid}
## Euclid's Algorithm
To compute $\gcd(a, b)$, replace $(a, b)$ with $(b, a \bmod b)$ until $b = 0$; the remaining $a$ is the gcd.
:::

::: {#def-prime}
## Prime
A positive integer $p > 1$ is prime if its only positive divisors are 1 and $p$.
:::

::: {#exm-six}
## Composite
$6 = 2 \times 3$, so $6$ is not prime.
:::

::: {#exr-factor}
## Factorize
Find the prime factorization of $360$.
:::

:::: {.content-hidden when-format="latex"}
::: {#prf-even}
## Proof
Write $n = 2k$; then $n^2 = 4k^2$.
:::
::::

::: {.proof}
Write $n = 2k$; then $n^2 = 4k^2$.
:::

::: {#rem-largest}
## Remark
The infinitude of primes was proved by Euclid around 300 BCE.
:::

::: {#sol-factor}
## Solution
$360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5$.
:::
